steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: The Functions KV store is the Post-it note pad at every desk. Stash a token, redirect rule, or feature flag — every Function instance worldwide reads it instantly.

steps.label: How It Works
Real World
steps.body:
Canonical use: managing 100,000+ redirect rules. Pre-Functions, this either sat on origin (slow) or hardcoded into the EdgeWorker (un-updateable without a deploy). With Functions KV: rules live in the store, the Function looks them up in microseconds, and updates propagate without a deploy.

Not a primary database — pair with Linode Managed Postgres for source of truth, treat KV as the read-fast cache.
"Functions KV = read-fast KV everywhere your Functions run. Cache, flags, redirects, tokens. Not a primary DB."

quiz.question: You're managing 1M URL redirect rules for a major site migration. Where do you store them?

options.explain: Every change becomes a deploy. Doesn't scale to 1M rules.

options.text: Functions KV store, looked up per request by the Function
options.explain: Built for this. Rules stored once, read in microseconds globally. Updates propagate without a deploy.
Adds latency on every request. KV is the right tier in front of Postgres.

options.text: In-memory only — load on cold start
options.explain: Loading 1M rules on every cold start is expensive. KV avoids it.

quiz.question: How does the Functions KV store relate to EdgeKV?

options.text: Functions KV is for Functions; EdgeKV is for EdgeWorkers — same idea (low-latency KV), different runtimes
options.explain: Both are read-fast KV stores. EdgeKV pairs with EdgeWorkers (PoP-level JS). Functions KV pairs with Akamai Functions (Wasm at core/distributed regions).
EdgeKV is still very much alive — it's the EdgeWorker companion.
KV is read-fast, not transactional. Pair with Postgres for source of truth.
